label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. So, the passage is about memory and how it can play tricks on us. The question asks which of the statements is true based on the passage. First, I need to look at the relevant part of the passage. The first example given is about the writer finding the front door unlocked in the morning, but remembering having locked it the night before. Let me read that part again: "One morning last week, for example, I got up and found that I had left the front door unlocked all night, yet I clearly remember locking it carefully the night before." So the situation is that the writer found the door unlocked, but thought they had locked it. So they actually didn't lock it (they forgot), but they remember doing so. Now let me look at the answer options: (A) One night the writer forgot to lock the front door. - This seems true because the door was actually left unlocked, which means they forgot to lock it. But let me check the other options. (B) One night the writer forgot having locked the front door. - This would mean they locked it but forgot that they did. But in the passage, the writer remembers having locked it (they "clearly remember locking it"), but in reality, the door was unlocked. So (B) is not correct. (C) The writer remembered to lock the door. - This is a bit ambiguous. If "remembered to lock" means they remembered the action and did it, but the passage says they didn't lock it. So (C) is incorrect. (D) the writer remembered unlocking the front door. - No, the writer remembered locking it, not unlocking. So (D) is wrong. Therefore, the
